description: Research and design a webhook notification system for wreck-it. The system should allow users to configure one or more webhook URLs (e.g. Slack, Discord, or generic HTTP endpoints) that receive JSON payloads whenever a task changes status (started, completed, failed). Review the existing Config and Task types in cli/src/types.rs and cli/src/ralph_loop.rs to design the integration points. Propose a Config field (e.g. notify_webhooks: Vec<String>), a minimal JSON payload schema, and identify exactly where in ralph_loop.rs the hook calls should be inserted (after status transitions). Document findings as inline comments in a new cli/src/notifier.rs stub file.

Implement the webhook notification system designed in ideas-webhook-notifications. Create cli/src/notifier.rs with an async `notify` function that sends an HTTP POST with a JSON payload (task id, status, timestamp, description) to each configured URL using reqwest. Add a `notify_webhooks: Vec<String>` field to Config in cli/src/types.rs (default empty, skip_serializing_if empty). Wire calls to `notify` in cli/src/ralph_loop.rs after task status transitions to InProgress, Completed, and Failed. Add `--notify-webhook <URL>` (multi-value) CLI flag in cli/src/cli.rs that appends to the config's webhook list. Ensure failures to reach a webhook are logged as warnings but do not abort the loop.

Add unit and integration tests for the webhook notification system. In cli/src/notifier.rs or a separate test module, use a mock HTTP server (wiremock or httpmock crate, or a simple tokio TCP listener) to verify: (1) a POST request is sent to each configured URL on task status change, (2) the JSON payload contains the correct task id, status, and timestamp fields, (3) a webhook failure (connection refused) logs a warning but does not propagate an error. Run `cargo test` to confirm all tests pass.

Evaluate the completed webhook notification implementation. Review cli/src/notifier.rs, the Config changes in cli/src/types.rs, the call sites in cli/src/ralph_loop.rs, and the CLI flag in cli/src/cli.rs. Verify: (1) the code compiles with no warnings (`cargo clippy -- -D warnings`), (2) the JSON payload schema is documented clearly, (3) errors are handled gracefully without aborting the loop, (4) the feature is reflected in README.md Features list. Fix any issues found and mark this task complete.

Research and design per-task timeout and auto-retry support for wreck-it. Review the Task struct in core/src/types.rs (or cli/src/types.rs) and the agent execution flow in cli/src/ralph_loop.rs and cli/src/agent.rs. Propose: (1) new optional Task fields `timeout_seconds: Option<u64>` and `max_retries: Option<u32>`, (2) how tokio::time::timeout wraps the agent call, (3) how the retry counter is tracked in task state without requiring a new state file (reuse or extend the existing failed_attempts field if present). Document the design with inline notes in a scratch section of this task's description or as comments in a new branch of the relevant source files.

Implement per-task timeout and auto-retry. Add optional fields `timeout_seconds: Option<u64>` and `max_retries: Option<u32>` to the Task struct in core/src/types.rs (with serde defaults of None). In cli/src/ralph_loop.rs, wrap the agent execution call with `tokio::time::timeout` when `timeout_seconds` is set, treating a timeout as a task failure with a clear error message. Add retry logic: if a task fails and its `failed_attempts` count is below `max_retries`, reset its status back to Pending (instead of Failed) so the scheduler picks it up again on the next iteration. Ensure retry counts are persisted to the task file.

Add tests for the per-task timeout and auto-retry feature. In core/src/ or cli/src/, add unit tests that verify: (1) a task with `timeout_seconds: 1` that takes longer than 1 second is marked as failed with a timeout error message, (2) a task with `max_retries: 2` that fails is reset to Pending status and only marked Failed after exceeding the retry limit, (3) a task with no timeout_seconds or max_retries behaves exactly as before. Run `cargo test` to confirm all tests pass.

Evaluate the timeout and auto-retry implementation. Review the Task struct changes, ralph_loop.rs modifications, and new tests. Verify: (1) `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes cleanly, (2) the task file format documentation in README.md is updated to document the new `timeout_seconds` and `max_retries` fields in the task format table, (3) retry semantics do not conflict with the existing adaptive re-planning feature (replan threshold). Fix any issues found.

Research and design a `wreck-it graph` sub-command that reads a task file and exports the dependency graph in Mermaid flowchart and/or GraphViz DOT format. Review the existing CLI structure in cli/src/cli.rs (clap commands) and the Task type. Design: (1) a new `graph` subcommand with flags `--task-file`, `--format <mermaid|dot>`, and `--output <file>` (default stdout), (2) the Mermaid flowchart syntax for representing task nodes (id + role) and directed edges (depends_on relationships), coloured by status (pending=gray, completed=green, failed=red). No new dependencies should be needed since graph generation is pure string formatting.

Implement the `wreck-it graph` sub-command. Add a `Graph` variant to the Commands enum in cli/src/cli.rs with args `--task-file`, `--format` (mermaid or dot, default mermaid), and `--output`. Create cli/src/graph.rs with a `generate_mermaid` and `generate_dot` function that reads the task list and emits the graph text: nodes labelled with task id and role, edges from each task to its dependents, node style reflecting status (pending/completed/failed/inprogress). Wire the command in cli/src/main.rs. No extra crate dependencies are needed.

Add tests for the graph export feature. In cli/src/graph.rs or a test module, add unit tests that: (1) verify `generate_mermaid` produces valid Mermaid syntax for a simple 3-task chain (A → B → C), including the correct flowchart header and arrow syntax, (2) verify `generate_dot` produces valid DOT syntax with digraph header, node definitions, and edge declarations, (3) verify tasks with no dependencies appear as isolated nodes. Run `cargo test` to confirm all tests pass.

Evaluate the graph export implementation. Review cli/src/graph.rs and cli/src/cli.rs changes. Verify: (1) `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes, (2) the `wreck-it graph` command is documented in README.md under Usage, (3) the Mermaid output renders correctly when pasted into mermaid.live, (4) edge cases are handled (tasks with no depends_on, circular deps emitting a warning). Fix any issues found.

Research and design GitHub Issues integration for wreck-it. The feature should optionally create a GitHub Issue when a task starts (InProgress) and close it when the task completes or fails. Review the existing CLI config (cli/src/types.rs Config struct), the project_api.rs and agent_memory.rs files, and the GitHub REST API for issue creation/closure. Design: (1) new Config fields `github_issues_enabled: bool`, `github_repo: Option<String>` (owner/repo), `github_token: Option<String>` (falls back to GITHUB_TOKEN env var), (2) a minimal GitHub API client in cli/src/github_client.rs using reqwest, (3) the label strategy (add a `wreck-it` label, use task id as issue title prefix). Document the design.

Implement GitHub Issues integration. Create cli/src/github_client.rs with async functions `create_issue(token, repo, title, body) -> Result<u64>` and `close_issue(token, repo, issue_number) -> Result<()>` using reqwest and the GitHub REST API (POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues, PATCH to close). Add Config fields `github_issues_enabled`, `github_repo`, `github_token`. Add CLI flags `--github-issues`, `--github-repo <owner/repo>`, `--github-token <TOKEN>`. Wire into ralph_loop.rs: call create_issue when a task moves to InProgress (store issue number in task metadata or a side-channel map), call close_issue with the appropriate state when it reaches Completed or Failed. Failures should log warnings but not abort the loop.

Add tests for the GitHub Issues integration. Mock the GitHub REST API (using httpmock or wiremock, or a simple tokio TCP server) to verify: (1) a POST to /repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues is made when a task transitions to InProgress, (2) a PATCH request to close the issue is made when the task reaches Completed or Failed, (3) when github_issues_enabled is false, no HTTP calls are made, (4) a GitHub API error (e.g. 401 Unauthorized) logs a warning and does not abort the loop. Run `cargo test` to confirm all tests pass.

Evaluate the GitHub Issues integration. Review cli/src/github_client.rs, Config changes, CLI flags, and ralph_loop.rs call sites. Verify: (1) `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes, (2) the feature is opt-in (no behavior change when github_issues_enabled is false or absent), (3) GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable fallback works correctly, (4) the feature is documented in README.md with a short description and the new CLI flags, (5) no sensitive tokens are logged at any level. Fix any issues found and mark the task complete.

Research and design an API token cost-tracking and budget-control system for wreck-it. Review the agent execution flow in cli/src/agent.rs and cli/src/ralph_loop.rs to identify where token usage can be captured from LLM API responses (usage field in OpenAI-compatible responses). Design: (1) a CostTracker struct that accumulates prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, and estimated_cost_usd per task and for the whole run, (2) a new Config field `max_cost_usd: Option<f64>` that aborts the loop when the cumulative estimated cost exceeds the threshold, (3) a cost summary line printed at the end of each iteration in both headless and TUI modes, (4) per-model pricing constants for common GitHub Models / OpenAI models. Document the design as inline comments in a new cli/src/cost_tracker.rs stub file.

Implement the API token cost-tracking system designed in ideas-api-cost-tracking. Create cli/src/cost_tracker.rs with a CostTracker struct holding per-task and cumulative token counts and a method to compute estimated_cost_usd given a model name. Extend the agent response handling in cli/src/agent.rs to capture the usage object from OpenAI-compatible API responses and update the tracker. Add `max_cost_usd: Option<f64>` to Config in cli/src/types.rs (default None, skip_serializing_if None). In cli/src/ralph_loop.rs, check the cumulative cost after each task and abort the loop with a clear message if max_cost_usd is exceeded. Add a `--max-cost <USD>` CLI flag in cli/src/cli.rs. Print a cost summary (total tokens + estimated USD) at the end of each iteration and at run completion.

Add unit and integration tests for the API cost-tracking feature. In cli/src/cost_tracker.rs or a test module, verify: (1) CostTracker accumulates token counts correctly across multiple tasks, (2) estimated_cost_usd is computed correctly for at least two known models with fixed per-token rates, (3) when max_cost_usd is set and the cumulative cost exceeds it, the loop-abort condition is triggered correctly, (4) when max_cost_usd is None, no budget check occurs. Run `cargo test` to confirm all tests pass.

Evaluate the API cost-tracking implementation. Review cli/src/cost_tracker.rs, Config changes, CLI flag, and ralph_loop.rs call sites. Verify: (1) `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes cleanly, (2) the cost summary output is human-readable and informative (tokens used, estimated USD), (3) the budget-abort message is clear and includes the current cost vs. limit, (4) the `max_cost_usd` Config field and `--max-cost` CLI flag are documented in README.md, (5) when the usage field is absent from an API response the tracker degrades gracefully (logs a warning, does not panic). Fix any issues found and mark the task complete.

Research and design a `wreck-it tasks` sub-command family for managing task files interactively from the CLI without editing JSON by hand. Review the existing CLI structure in cli/src/cli.rs (clap commands) and the Task type in cli/src/types.rs. Design sub-commands: (1) `wreck-it tasks list [--task-file] [--status pending|inprogress|completed|failed]` — tabular output of all tasks with their status and role, (2) `wreck-it tasks add --id <id> --description <desc> [--role ideas|implementer|evaluator] [--phase N] [--priority N] [--depends-on id1,id2]` — appends a new task to the file, (3) `wreck-it tasks set-status --id <id> --status <status>` — updates the status of a task in place, (4) `wreck-it tasks validate [--task-file]` — checks for missing required fields, duplicate IDs, and unresolved depends_on references. Document design as inline comments in cli/src/cli.rs.

Implement the `wreck-it tasks` sub-command family designed in ideas-task-cli-management. Add a `Tasks` variant to the Commands enum in cli/src/cli.rs with `list`, `add`, `set-status`, and `validate` sub-commands and their respective argument structs. Create cli/src/task_cli.rs implementing each sub-command: list prints a formatted table (id, role, phase, status, depends_on count), add reads the task file, appends the new task, and writes it back, set-status mutates the matching task's status field in place, validate checks for duplicate IDs and unresolved depends_on chains and reports errors. Wire the `Tasks` command in cli/src/main.rs. No new crate dependencies beyond what is already available (use the existing serde_json and tabular-style formatting with plain std string formatting).

Add tests for the `wreck-it tasks` sub-commands. In cli/src/task_cli.rs or a test module, write unit tests that: (1) verify `list` correctly filters by status and formats task rows, (2) verify `add` appends a valid new task to a JSON file and that the resulting file parses correctly, (3) verify `set-status` updates the correct task and leaves all other tasks unchanged, (4) verify `validate` detects and reports duplicate IDs and a depends_on reference to a nonexistent task, (5) verify `validate` returns a clean result for a well-formed task file. Run `cargo test` to confirm all tests pass.

Evaluate the `wreck-it tasks` CLI implementation. Review cli/src/task_cli.rs and cli/src/cli.rs changes. Verify: (1) `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes, (2) each sub-command is documented in README.md under Usage, (3) error messages for invalid input (unknown task id, invalid status value) are user-friendly, (4) the `add` command validates that the provided id does not already exist and reports an error if it does, (5) the `validate` sub-command is suitable for use in CI scripts (exits non-zero on errors). Fix any issues found and mark the task complete.

Research and design multi-repository orchestration for wreck-it. The feature should allow a single `wreck-it run` invocation to coordinate tasks that span multiple local git repositories (e.g., a monorepo split into separate repos, or a frontend + backend pair). Review the Config and RalphConfig types in cli/src/types.rs and cli/src/repo_config.rs, the agent execution in cli/src/agent.rs, and the git integration in cli/src/state_worktree.rs. Design: (1) a new optional `work_dirs: HashMap<String, String>` Config field mapping task roles or ids to repo paths, (2) how the agent context (file listing, git diff) is assembled when a task's work_dir differs from the root, (3) how test-runner detection (cargo/npm/pytest) works per-repo, (4) how commits are made to the correct repo. Document the design in a new docs/multi-repo.md stub.

Implement the multi-repository orchestration feature designed in ideas-multi-repo-orchestration. Add `work_dirs: HashMap<String, String>` to Config in cli/src/types.rs (maps task-id prefix or role to a local path; absent means use the default work_dir). In cli/src/ralph_loop.rs, resolve the effective work_dir for each task before dispatching it to the agent. In cli/src/agent.rs, accept a per-call work_dir override so that file context, test running, and git commits all use the resolved path. Add a `--work-dir-map <ROLE_OR_ID>=<PATH>` CLI flag (multi-value) in cli/src/cli.rs that populates the config map. Create the docs/multi-repo.md documentation file explaining the feature and providing an example config.

Add tests for the multi-repository orchestration feature. In cli/src/ or a test module, write tests using temporary directories to verify: (1) when `work_dirs` maps a task id to a secondary path, the agent receives the correct work_dir for that task while other tasks use the default, (2) git commits are made to the correct repository (not the default one) when a task is mapped to a different path, (3) when `work_dirs` is empty or absent, behavior is identical to the current single-repo behavior. Run `cargo test` to confirm all tests pass.

Evaluate the multi-repository orchestration implementation. Review the Config changes, ralph_loop.rs modifications, agent.rs work_dir override, and docs/multi-repo.md. Verify: (1) `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes, (2) the feature is fully backward-compatible when `work_dirs` is absent, (3) docs/multi-repo.md contains a clear example showing a two-repo setup, (4) the `--work-dir-map` CLI flag is documented in README.md under Usage, (5) path validation (work_dir must be an existing directory and a git repo) produces a clear error message. Fix any issues found and mark the task complete.
